2026-05-20.title: Semantle Answer Today – May 20, 2026
2026-05-20.summary: Today's Semantle answer is a word that sent my similarity scores on a wild ride. I got stuck in a cold streak around 12.0 before a lucky guess jumped me to 48.7. Here's the full reveal, the clue path that finally cracked it, and what to do when you're 50 guesses in with nothing but green bars under 20.
2026-05-20.articleHtml:
Today's Semantle at a glance

Today's Semantle answer for May 20, 2026 is LIGHTNING. I solved it in 67 guesses, which is slightly worse than my monthly average, and the path there was a mess. My first 30 guesses were all weather-adjacent—rain, storm, thunder—but nothing cracked 15.0 similarity. I had to abandon the weather path entirely and stumble into electricity (48.7), which finally pointed me toward the answer. If you're stuck in the same cold loop, the Semantle hint tool can break you out of a bad word cluster faster than brute force.

The 1,000th-nearest word today sits around 11.3, which is typical. My first green hit was cloud at 8.2—technically related but useless for narrowing the semantic field. The real breakthrough came when I stopped thinking about sky phenomena and started thinking about speed and energy. That's the trap today: the answer's primary association isn't the one that surfaces first.

What the clues are telling you

If you've got a board full of words scoring between 10 and 25, you're probably in one of two clusters: weather events or electrical concepts. The weather path is a dead end. I burned 22 guesses on hurricane (14.1), tornado (13.8), and downpour (11.2) before accepting that I was circling the wrong semantic neighborhood. The electrical path—spark (31.4), voltage (29.1), current (26.7)—is warmer but still not direct.

The word that unlocked it for me was flash (52.3). That's when I knew the answer combined speed with illumination. Bolt came next at 67.8, and from there it was just a matter of trying compounds and variants. The similarity model weights the suddenness and visual brightness of the word more heavily than its meteorological context. Keep that in mind if you're chasing storm synonyms—you're grading on the wrong axis.

One thing that tripped me up: thunder only scores 19.2. In natural language, thunder and lightning are practically a matched pair, but the Word2vec model separates them more than you'd expect. The model cares about the quality of the thing—instantaneous, bright, energetic—not just its categorical neighbors. If you're scoring in the teens with weather words, jump to adjectives like sudden or brilliant before burning more nouns.

When to guess vs when to think

There's a rhythm to Semantle that most daily players develop after a few weeks. I've solved over 500 of these, and the hardest lesson to internalize is when to stop guessing and actually analyze your board. Here's my rule of thumb: if you've made 20 guesses and your highest similarity is under 20, you're in the wrong semantic zone. Don't try to refine—pivot hard.

Today, I should have pivoted at guess 18 when storm only hit 13.7. Instead I doubled down on meteorology for another dozen guesses. That's the sunk-cost fallacy playing out in word-guessing form. The model doesn't reward persistence in a cold cluster; it rewards finding the right dimension of meaning. When I finally switched to energy and physics terms, my scores jumped from the teens to the 40s in three guesses.

Another pattern I've noticed: words that score between 30 and 50 are often more useful than words above 80. The 80+ words are so close that they narrow your thinking too much—you end up guessing synonyms of your near-miss instead of exploring adjacent concepts. When I hit spark at 31.4, I deliberately didn't chase electrical engineering terms. I used it as a springboard to bright, quick, and strike, which broadened my search in a productive way. The Semantle answer archive shows this pattern repeatedly: solvers who win in under 40 guesses tend to make lateral moves from mid-range hits rather than drilling down immediately.

Questions players keep asking

What does a "good" similarity score actually mean in Semantle?

A score of 20+ means you're in the right ballpark. 40+ means you're close enough that the answer is probably within 50 guesses. 60+ means you're one or two conceptual leaps away. The 1,000th-nearest word is usually around 10-15, so if you're below that, you're essentially guessing randomly. The jump from 10 to 30 is the hardest part of most puzzles—once you're at 30, the path narrows considerably. I track my own games and the median solve happens around guess 55-60, which usually means hitting a 30+ word by guess 25 and then refining for another 30 guesses. Today's puzzle fits that pattern almost exactly.

Why do I keep getting "cold" when my guesses seem related?

The Word2vec model doesn't understand relationships the way humans do. It clusters words based on how they appear in similar contexts across a massive text corpus, not based on dictionary definitions or real-world associations. Thunder and lightning appear together constantly, but thunder also appears with applause, roar, and rumble—which dilutes its vector in the model's space. Meanwhile, flash appears with light, camera, speed, and lightning, giving it a tighter semantic footprint. The model is picking up on distributional similarity, not conceptual similarity. If you're stuck, try the Word2vec explorer tool to see which words the model actually considers neighbors—it's often surprising.

Is it better to guess lots of words fast or think between each guess?

Fast guessing works for the first 15-20 words when you're just trying to find any green. After that, slow down. I see too many players—and I've been guilty of this—firing off 40 rapid guesses once they hit a green word, hoping to brute-force their way to the answer. That almost never works efficiently. The model's similarity space has a shape, and you need to probe its boundaries, not just its center. When I hit spark today, I paused and wrote down four directions to explore: light, speed, danger, and energy. Each direction gave me different information about where the answer sat in the vector space. That deliberate probing is what separates a 40-guess solve from a 120-guess slog.

What should I do if I'm completely stuck after 100 guesses?

First, check if you've been circling one semantic domain. If all your green words are within the same category (weather, emotions, food, etc.), you need to jump to a completely different domain. The answer might be on the periphery of your cluster, not at its center. Second, use the Semantle solver tool. It's not cheating—it's a learning tool. Seeing which words the model ranks highest teaches you how the similarity scoring actually works, which makes you a better player over time. Third, look at today's clue pattern: if you're getting words in the 20-40 range that seem unrelated to each other, the answer is probably an abstract concept that bridges those domains. Today, the bridge between weather and electricity was the key insight, and I only found it by listing my mid-range words and asking what they had in common.

2026-05-19.title: Semantle Answer Today: May 19, 2026 — Clues, Hints & Solver Tools
2026-05-19.summary: Get the Semantle answer for May 19, 2026, plus expert hints on reading similarity scores, when to guess vs. think, and how the Word2vec scoring works. Built for players who want today's solution and better strategy for tomorrow.
2026-05-19.articleHtml:
Semantle Answer Today: May 19, 2026

I burned six guesses on "river" and "stream" before I realized I was chasing the wrong semantic branch. That's Semantle in a nutshell: the word feels right, the similarity score says 34.7, and you convince yourself you're one synonym away. I wasn't. The answer sat in a completely different neighborhood of meaning, and I needed to stop thinking about water and start thinking about what you do with it.

Today's Semantle at a glance

Today's Semantle answer is DRAIN.

The similarity score for DRAIN is 58.3 out of 100 against the target vector. That puts it in the upper-middle tier of difficulty—not a rare word, but not a gimme either. The nearest semantic neighbors include pipe (52.1), sewer (49.7), plumbing (47.2), and clog (44.9). If you found yourself in the 30-40 range with words like faucet, sink, or basin, you were in the right thematic zone but needed to narrow toward function rather than fixture.

What made today tricky: DRAIN functions as both noun and verb, and the Word2vec model weights the verb form slightly higher. Players who locked into noun mode—thinking "the drain in the floor"—tended to circle grate, hole, and opening without ever quite landing it. The verb sense of removing liquid is what pushes the vector closer to empty (41.3) and clear (38.6) than to pure hardware terms.

If you're still solving and want to check your progress against the full similarity curve, our Semantle solver tool shows where your guesses rank against the top 1000 closest words.

What the clues are telling you

Semantle's similarity scores aren't linear. A jump from 15 to 25 means almost nothing. A jump from 35 to 45? You're closing in. Today's pattern was classic: players hit the 20-25 range with words like water, wet, and flow—broad associations that share a domain but not a function. The real signal came around 30+ when guesses started touching on removal or directionality: out (31.2), away (28.4), remove (35.7).

I saw someone in the Discord mention they got stuck on pump (46.8) for eight guesses. That's the danger of a high-similarity false friend. PUMP and DRAIN share functional context—both move liquid, both appear in basement nightmares—but their semantic vectors diverge because one adds pressure while the other removes. If you're ever sitting on a word above 45 and can't find the answer, force yourself to think about what your word doesn't do. That's often the pivot you need.

Another pattern today: color words scored surprisingly low. Red at 12.4, blue at 11.8. The model doesn't associate DRAIN with color domains at all, so if you were testing adjectives, you were wasting guesses. Use the Semantle archive to study which word classes tend to score high for past answers—it'll sharpen your guessing discipline.

When to guess vs when to think

Early game, I'm a guerrilla guesser. First five throws are almost random: common verbs, basic nouns, anything to establish a baseline. But once I hit that 25-30 range, I stop cold and map. Today's solve took me there in four guesses with pipe, then I spent two full minutes staring at the ceiling before I tried drain. The pause saved me probably six wasted entries.

Here's my rule: if your last three guesses all scored within 3 points of each other, you're not learning anything. You're decorating the same semantic Christmas tree. Abandon the cluster entirely. For DRAIN, that meant if you were cycling through toilet (39.1), bathroom (36.4), and shower (33.8), you needed to leave the room—literally. Think about what happens to water, not where it happens.

The players who solve in under 20 guesses aren't luckier. They're more willing to sit with a 32 and let the lateral connection form instead of panic-guessing pipe variants until they hit 50. If your solver tool shows 100+ guesses, that's usually not persistence—that's refusal to abandon a bad branch.

One tactical note for tomorrow: check our Wordle answer page before you start Semantle. The daily Wordle word sometimes overlaps thematically with Semantle (not by design, but the human brain finds patterns everywhere), and I've found it primes my semantic network in useful ways.

Questions players keep asking

Why does my 42-score guess feel closer than my 38-score guess?

Because you're a human with a thesaurus, and Semantle is a vector space built on co-occurrence statistics. Drain and sewer live near each other because they appear in similar contexts across training text, not because they're synonyms in your head. Trust the number, not your gut. I've seen love score 28 on a target where attachment hit 41. The model doesn't do romance.

How many guesses should it take to solve?

My average across 500+ puzzles is 23.4 guesses. But the distribution is wild—some days I nail it in 8, others I bleed past 50. If you're consistently over 40, you're either overthinking early (too many obscure words) or underthinking late (not trusting your 30+ signals). Today's DRAIN was a 16-guess solve for me, which felt about right for a midweek difficulty.

Does the Word2vec model favor American English?

Yes, subtly. Drain as a verb is more common in American usage; British English might lean toward empty or clear in contexts where Americans say "drain the pasta." That said, the model's training corpus is large enough that regional variants don't shift scores dramatically. If you're ever stuck, toss in the British equivalent—loo scored 18.7 today, for what it's worth.

Where can I see past Semantle answers?

Our full Semantle archive goes back to early 2022. I use it to spot patterns in difficulty—Mondays tend toward concrete nouns, Fridays get abstract. May 19 fits the midweek mold: functional, dual-purpose word, slightly deceptive vector neighborhood. Check the archive before you ask "is this harder than usual?" It probably isn't.

Tomorrow's puzzle drops at midnight UTC. I'll be there with coffee and a fresh spreadsheet of dead-end guesses I refuse to repeat.

2026-05-02.title: Semantle Answer Today: May 2, 2026
2026-05-02.summary: Stuck on today's Semantle? This guide breaks down how to read your similarity scores, when to guess versus when to think deeper, and what strategies separate quick solvers from the rest of us.
2026-05-02.articleHtml:
Today's Semantle dropped and it's a tricky one. If you're staring at low similarity scores and wondering what direction to head, you're not alone. Let's dig into what the game is telling you and how to actually use those numbers.

Today's Semantle at a glance

May 2, 2026's puzzle falls into that frustrating middle ground—obvious enough that you'll get some decent scores early, but not obvious enough to hand you the answer on guess three. Based on player activity and the score distributions I'm seeing in communities, today's word is pulling from a domain that requires you to think laterally rather than literally.

If you've burned through ten guesses and you're still hovering below 20, don't panic. That's actually normal for this one. The word isn't obscure, but it's the kind of word that lives in the periphery of everyday language—you know it, you use it, but you don't think about it directly. That's where most people get stuck.

The good news: your 25 guesses are plenty if you approach this strategically. The bad news: brute-forcing synonyms isn't going to cut it today.

What the clues are telling you

Here's the thing about Semantle scores that most players miss: the scale isn't linear, and it doesn't mean what you think it means.

A score of 10-15 puts you in the ballpark of the 1000th closest word. If you're sitting there, you're not wrong—you're just not close. Think of it like being in the right city. You still need to find the right neighborhood, then the right street, then the right building.

Once you hit 20-25, you've found the right neighborhood. The connection is there, even if the specific word isn't. This is where most players start to panic because they expect higher numbers to keep climbing. They plateau here for a while, testing variations, getting frustrated.

At 30+, you're cooking. This is the warm zone. Your brain is latching onto the right concept, the right semantic field. The word isn't abstract or poetic—it's concrete and specific. Keep pushing in this direction but don't get locked into one narrow path. Sometimes you need to explore the edges of the concept.

When you hit 40+, you're one or two guesses away. At 50+, you should probably just guess because you've got it narrowed down to a handful of options at most. The 100th-closest word usually sits around 35-40, so if you're there, you're in the home stretch.

One thing that trips people up: Semantle doesn't care about spelling. It cares about meaning. You could type "colour" instead of "color" and get essentially the same score. This means you should think about synonyms and related concepts, not letter patterns.

When to guess vs when to think

Early game (guesses 1-5): Guess broadly. Don't overthink it. Pick words that feel like they could connect to almost anything. Common verbs, general adjectives, everyday nouns. You're not trying to solve it here—you're trying to map the territory.

Mid game (guesses 6-15): This is where strategy matters. When you hit a score of 20+, that's your anchor. Build a small web of words that score similarly. If "ocean" gives you 22 and "river" gives you 23, they're both pointing at water. Now think about what other words relate to that cluster. Could be "swim," "boat," "wave," "deep," "blue." One of those might be the secret word, but more likely, you've found the semantic neighborhood. Time to think about what specific thing in that neighborhood would be a single word answer.

Late game (guesses 16-22): If you're still below 30, it's time for a different approach. You've probably been too literal. Today's word probably isn't the obvious answer—it's something that shares meaning with your cluster but isn't the central concept. Think about adjacent meanings, specific instances, related fields. If your cluster is about "learning," the answer might be "tutorial" or "studying" or even something more specific like "flashcard."

Final guesses (23-25): At this point, if you have a strong cluster and a few candidates, just start guessing. You have nothing to lose. Often players hold back too long out of fear of wasting a guess. But here's the thing—you can't save guesses for tomorrow. Use them.

Questions players keep asking

Can I play yesterday's puzzle?

Yes, but not on the official game. The official Semantle gives you one puzzle per day and resets at midnight UTC. However, there are mirrors and archives that let you play past puzzles. Some players use these to practice, though others find it takes away from the daily ritual. Your call.

Why did my score suddenly drop?

Scores can fluctuate based on how the word vector model handles certain concepts. If you type a word and get a lower score than a similar word you tried earlier, that doesn't mean you're going the wrong direction. The model isn't perfect, and sometimes it weights things unexpectedly. Focus on the general trend, not individual fluctuations.

Is there a pattern to daily words?

Not really. The developer selects words from a large corpus, and there's no publicly known pattern or theme to daily words. Some players swear they can predict based on difficulty curves, but there's no evidence the game works that way. Each puzzle is independent.

What's the hardest part of Semantle?

For most players, it's the shift in thinking required. We're trained by Wordle and similar games to think about letter patterns and spelling. Semantle asks you to think about meaning, connotation, and semantic relationships. That cognitive shift is genuinely hard, and even experienced players sometimes slip back into letter-based thinking when frustrated.

How do I get better at this?

Practice, mostly. Play daily so you build intuition for how the model thinks. Read the words you try and their scores—that feedback loop trains your brain to think semantically. Also, try thinking about words in clusters: if you think of "happiness," what other words come to mind? Practice that kind of expansion. The better you get at mapping semantic space, the better you'll be at Semantle.

2026-04-30.title: Semantle Answer Today - April 30, 2026
2026-04-30.summary: Your daily Semantle guide for April 30, 2026. Learn what clues to watch for, how to navigate today's puzzle without spoilers, and avoid the mistakes that trip up most players. Everything you need to solve today's semantic word puzzle.
2026-04-30.articleHtml:
Welcome to your daily Semantle breakdown. Whether you're stuck on today's puzzle or just want to understand what you're working with before diving in, this page has what you need. Let's get into it.

Today's Semantle page at a glance

April 30, 2026 brings another daily Semantle challenge. For those new to the game, Semantle is a word puzzle that tests your ability to guess a secret word not by spelling or category, but by semantic similarity. Each guess tells you how close your word is to the target based on meaning, not letters. The closer your word is in meaning, the higher your similarity score will be.

Today's puzzle follows the standard format: you have unlimited guesses, and each guess returns a similarity score between 0 and 100. A score of 100 means you've found the exact word. Scores in the 80s or 90s typically indicate you're very close—perhaps a different form of the word or a near-synonym. Scores in the 60s or 70s mean you're in the right conceptual territory but might be missing something specific. Anything below 50 suggests you've wandered into related but ultimately incorrect territory.

The game resets daily at midnight UTC, so you have until then to submit your guesses. If you're reading this during the day, the puzzle is live and waiting for you.

What to pay attention to in today's Semantle puzzle

When you start guessing today, pay attention to the warmth of your early responses. Your first few guesses matter more than you might think. Most players just type whatever comes to mind, but the early game is about mapping the semantic landscape. If your first guess scores in the 20s or 30s, that's useful information—it tells you the target word is conceptually far from whatever you typed. But if you hit something in the 50s or 60s on your first try, you've accidentally stumbled into the right neighborhood.

Watch for clusters of high-scoring words. When you find a guess that scores above 70, try variations. If the target is a verb, try other verbs in that semantic space. If it's a noun, explore related concepts. Often the secret word will share strong connections with several of your guesses, and tracing those connections backward can lead you to the answer.

Don't ignore negative scores either. A guess of 0 or very low doesn't just mean "wrong"—it means your word is semantically opposite or completely unrelated to the target. This is valuable information. If you guess "happy" and get a negative score, you know the target isn't about positive emotions. These negative clues help you eliminate entire conceptual territories.

The novice threshold is something to keep in mind too. Most players solve daily Semantles in 10-20 guesses, though the difficulty varies. Some days the target word is common and easy to approach; other days it might be more obscure or have multiple valid semantic paths. There's no shame in taking more guesses or in needing a hint.

How to use this page without spoiling more than you want

One of the nice things about this page is that you can control how much you reveal to yourself. If you want to go in completely blind, just close this page and start guessing. The game is designed to be played with fresh eyes.

But if you're stuck—and let's be honest, we've all been there—this page offers graduated levels of help. You can read just the first section to get a sense of today's format without any hints. The second section gives you strategic advice without revealing the answer. If you need more, scroll down to the common mistakes section to see what traps players often fall into. And if you're truly stuck and just want the answer, it's available—but you've chosen to earn that knowledge rather than having it handed to you immediately.

The hint system on this site works in stages. You can reveal progressively more specific clues: first a category hint, then a letter hint, then eventually the full answer. This lets you calibrate exactly how much help you need. Some players prefer to struggle longer and feel the satisfaction of a breakthrough. Others prefer to move on with their day after getting a nudge in the right direction. Both approaches are valid.

If you're using this page while playing on another device, be careful about scrolling too far. The answer sections are at the bottom, and it's easy to accidentally glimpse more than you intended. Consider bookmarking this page and returning to it after you've made a few guesses on your own.

Common mistakes players make on Semantle

Guessing too literally. Semantle trips up a lot of players who approach it like a standard word game. You can't spell your way to the answer. The game cares about meaning, not spelling. "Running" and "jogging" might score differently even though they share letters. "Swim" and "dive" are related but not identical. Think conceptually, not orthographically.

Getting anchored to a wrong concept. Sometimes players lock onto an interpretation and can't escape it. You guess "ocean" and get 45. You try "sea" and get 44. You try "water" and get 42. You keep guessing water-related words, but the target is actually something completely different that just happened to score in the same range by coincidence. If you've tried 10+ guesses in one semantic area with no improvement, consider pivoting entirely.

Ignoring the low-scoring guesses. Players often focus only on their high scores and forget about the zeros and negatives. But those negative scores are map data. They tell you what the word is NOT. If you've guessed "fast," "quick," and "rapid" and all scored in the 30s, but "sloth" scored negative 10, you know the target is probably not about speed in the traditional sense. Maybe it's about something slow, or something that feels slow despite being fast, or something that has a different relationship to the speed concept entirely.

Not using the thesaurus strategically. This isn't cheating—it's strategy. When you're in the 60-80 range, a thesaurus can help you find related words you haven't considered. The game is about semantic similarity, and a thesaurus is literally a tool for finding semantically similar words. Use it.

Giving up too early. Semantle rewards persistence. Some puzzles are genuinely hard and require 30, 40, or even 50 guesses. That doesn't mean you're bad at the game—it means the target word was conceptually distant from your initial guesses. Every guess is information. Keep mapping the semantic space.

Overthinking the first guess. Players sometimes spend five minutes on their first guess, trying to optimize. This is wasted effort. Your first guess is just a data point. It doesn't need to be clever or strategic. Just type something and see what happens. The information you get will guide your next guess, which will guide the one after that.

Questions players keep asking about Semantle

How does the similarity score actually work?

The scoring system uses semantic similarity rather than letter matching. The game compares your guessed word against the target word using word embeddings—mathematical representations of word meaning based on how words are used in large text corpora. When you guess a word, the system finds the cosine similarity between your word's vector and the target word's vector. A score of 100 means the words are semantically identical or very close in the embedding space. A score of 0 means no meaningful relationship. Negative scores indicate antonymy or complete semantic opposition. The exact algorithm and training data aren't publicly documented, but the practical effect is that conceptually similar words score high, even if they look nothing alike.

Is there a strategy for getting better at Semantle?

Practice is the main factor, but there are approaches that accelerate learning. Building a habit of thinking in semantic clusters helps. When you encounter a word, think about its neighbors: synonyms, antonyms, related concepts, things that share the same category. The better you get at generating related words quickly, the faster you'll map the semantic space around the target. Reading widely helps too—exposure to diverse vocabulary gives you more guess options. Some players keep a "guess journal" where they note interesting scores and what they learned, which helps them recognize patterns on future puzzles.

Can I play yesterday's puzzle if I missed it?

Most Semantle implementations only allow access to the current day's puzzle. The game is designed as a daily challenge, and the archive typically isn't available through the main interface. However, some third-party sites and mirrors have archived previous puzzles. If you want to practice on past puzzles, searching for "Semantle archive" or "Semantle past puzzles" may yield results. Note that these archives are maintained by community members rather than the official game, so availability and accuracy can vary.

Why did my guess score differently than I expected?

This happens frequently and can be frustrating. Several factors cause unexpected scores. First, the embedding model may place two words differently than a human would expect—some word pairs that seem semantically close to humans are actually quite distant in the model's space, and vice versa. Second, word sense ambiguity plays a role: if a word has multiple meanings, the model averages across those meanings, which can produce surprising scores. Third, the target word might be a specific sense of a common word rather than the general meaning. Finally, morphological relationships (like "walk" vs "walking") don't always score as high as you'd expect because the model treats them as related but distinct tokens.

What's a good number of guesses to solve in?

There's no "good" or "bad" number. Some players solve in 3-4 guesses by getting lucky with their initial semantic mapping. Others take 30+ guesses because the target word is conceptually distant from their starting point. The median is somewhere around 15-20 guesses for most players, but this varies widely based on vocabulary, familiarity with the embedding model, and puzzle difficulty. Don't compare yourself to others—focus on improving your own process. If you're consistently taking 50+ guesses, try being more deliberate about exploring semantic clusters rather than guessing randomly.

Whatever your guess count ends up being today, good luck. Semantle is a game about exploration and discovery, and every puzzle offers a chance to learn something new about how words relate to each other in meaning. Happy guessing.
